name: NIKE, Inc.

profile.description: NIKE, Inc. is the world's largest athletic footwear and apparel company, designing, marketing, and distributing products under the Nike, Jordan, and Converse brands in over 190 countries. Under CEO Elliott Hill — a 32-year Nike veteran who returned in October 2024 — the company is executing a turnaround strategy after losing market share to On Running and Hoka. FY2026 TTM revenue is $46.5B with significant margin pressure from $1.5B in annual tariff headwinds.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): Nike's running category grew 20%+ for two consecutive quarters driven by Nike Mind (10+ years in development) and the NikeSkims partnership launch, but the company has not executed a transformative business model pivot — it remains a traditional athletic footwear/apparel company losing share to On Running and Hoka [1]. AI Deployment (1/2): Nike created a Chief AI Officer role and is integrating AI into generative design tools, supply chain optimization, and personalized marketing, but no quantified AI-driven revenue gains or cost savings have been publicly reported. VP of AI departed the company in 2025 [2]. Market Share (1/2): Nike remains the world's largest athletic brand by revenue ($46.5B TTM) but is actively losing share in key running and lifestyle categories to competitors. NIKE Direct declined 9% and Converse fell 30% in Q2 FY2026 [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Nike Mind technology platform and NikeSkims collaboration provide near-term product visibility, but innovation chief Tony Bignell departed in April 2026 and was replaced by Andy Caine, raising execution questions on the pipeline [2]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Nike's $5B+ annual marketing investment and substantial product development spending are above athletic industry peers as a percentage of revenue [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Nike's mission — 'to bring inspiration and innovation to every athlete in the world (*if you have a body, you are an athlete)' — is specific, inclusive, service-oriented, and validated through 60 years of execution across running, basketball, soccer, and lifestyle categories [1]. The mission has survived labor controversies, leadership transitions, and the Kaepernick era while consistently shaping product and marketing decisions. Falls just short of a 4 because the 'Win Now' turnaround framing signals short-term financial focus. Community Impact (1/2): Nike Foundation supports youth athletics through NikeGO and community investment programs in underserved communities, with genuine charitable commitment, though specific dollar amounts for recent giving are not prominently disclosed [3]. Employee Retention (0/2): Consecutive rounds of layoffs — 1,600 in 2024, corporate cuts August 2025, 775 distribution workers January 2026 — plus departures of the innovation chief and AI VP indicate organizational instability that undermines retention [2]. No evidence of above-average tenure or low voluntary turnover. Insider Buying (2/2): CEO Elliott Hill purchased $2M in NKE shares in April 2026, Director Timothy Cook bought $1.06M, Director Robert Swan bought $500K, and Director John Rogers bought $173K — meaningful net insider buying totaling $3.7M+ by multiple executives and directors [4].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as a dividend stock. D/E of 79.3% is near the 70% target (score 0.96). ROE of 16% just exceeds 15% target (score 1.0). Revenue growth YoY of 0.1% is anemic (score 0.67). 3-year revenue CAGR of -0.3% reflects contraction from FY2022-FY2025 (score 0.31) [1]. Forward P/E of 24.3x is above 20x target but reasonable (score 0.89). Profit margin of 4.8% is severely compressed from historical 12%+ by $1.5B tariff headwinds and restructuring costs (score 0.24) [1]. Payout ratio of 107% exceeds 100% floor (score 0.0) — dividend temporarily exceeding earnings during the trough. Consecutive years paid of 22+ (score 1.0, 2x weight) and consecutive years growth of 22+ (score 1.0) demonstrate elite dividend commitment. 5-year dividend growth CAGR of 8.3% exceeds 6% target (score 1.0, 2x weight). Weighted sum 9.08/12, Infrastructure = 8. Liquidity passes at $1.02B daily [4].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Nike's core business — designing, manufacturing, and marketing physical athletic footwear and apparel — is protected from AI disruption. Consumers buy Nike for the brand, performance innovation, and cultural relevance, none of which AI can substitute [1]. The brand moat built over 60 years with $5B annual marketing spend is extremely difficult for AI-enabled competitors to replicate. However, AI does pose moderate risk by enabling smaller competitors (On Running uses AI in shoe design) to close the product innovation gap, and AI-powered marketing platforms could allow DTC brands to match Nike's personalization at lower cost [2]. AI Opportunity (0/1): Despite creating a Chief AI Officer role and deploying AI across design, supply chain, and marketing, Nike has not reported quantifiable AI-generated revenue. The departure of the VP of AI in 2025 and the leadership overhaul under Hill suggest AI strategy is still being defined rather than monetized [2].

infinite_game.summary: Nike demonstrates a tension between infinite and finite mindsets. The 60-year mission of inspiring every athlete is genuinely transformative and has survived multiple crises, but the explicit 'Win Now' turnaround strategy signals finite, quarter-focused thinking that Sinek would view skeptically. CEO Hill's courageous decisions — re-embracing wholesale, $5B marketing investment during revenue decline — show long-term conviction, but consecutive layoffs and leadership turnover have eroded organizational trust.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Nike's mission to 'bring inspiration and innovation to every athlete in the world' is genuine, inclusive, and validated through 60 years of consistent execution that democratized athletic aspiration globally [1]. The cause survived the Kaepernick controversy, labor scandals, and multiple CEO transitions. However, the current 'Win Now' branding explicitly uses finite language — Sinek would note that 'winning' is the opposite of infinite thinking. The cause exists and is deeply rooted, but the current leadership is framing decisions around quarterly performance rather than the enduring mission.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Consecutive rounds of layoffs in 2024, 2025, and 2026 across corporate, distribution, and creative roles have severely damaged organizational trust [2]. Innovation chief Tony Bignell departed, VP of AI left, CTO role was eliminated, and the leadership team has been overhauled multiple times under Hill's restructuring. Glassdoor reviews reflect average-to-negative sentiment. When employees see colleagues cut quarter after quarter, psychological safety erodes regardless of stated purpose [2].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Hill's turnaround strategy is explicitly benchmarked against On Running and Hoka's product innovation in running, Adidas's cultural relevance recovery, and DTC competitors' digital capabilities [1]. Nike rejoined Amazon and rebuilt Foot Locker and Dick's partnerships in direct response to learning from competitive dynamics. Management language focuses on what rivals do better rather than on defeating them — a textbook infinite competitive mindset.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Nike demonstrated meaningful flexibility by reversing the Donahoe-era DTC-first strategy and re-embracing wholesale distribution under Hill, effectively admitting the prior strategy was wrong and pivoting the business model [1]. The NikeSkims partnership represents willingness to enter new market segments. However, these are course corrections within the existing athletic goods business rather than transformative reinventions — the core model of designing and marketing shoes/apparel has not fundamentally changed since 1964.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Hill publicly repudiated his predecessor's DTC-first strategy and re-embraced wholesale partners, requiring extraordinary institutional courage to admit a multi-year strategic direction was wrong [1]. Investing $5B+ in marketing during a year of declining revenues and compressing margins to restore brand relevance is precisely the kind of short-term sacrifice for long-term capability that Sinek celebrates. Multiple insiders buying $3.7M+ in shares during the stock's decline from $100+ to $42 demonstrates personal financial conviction [4].

investment_thesis: Nike at $44.19 (down 56% from 2024 highs) offers a contrarian value opportunity in the world's most recognizable athletic brand with a 3.6% dividend yield supported by 22 consecutive years of growth. CEO Elliott Hill's turnaround is showing early signs: running category grew 20%+ for two consecutive quarters, wholesale partnerships with Foot Locker and Dick's are being rebuilt, and $3.7M+ in insider buying by CEO Hill and directors Cook, Swan, and Rogers in April 2026 signals conviction at these levels [1][4]. The 24x forward P/E reflects trough earnings — normalized margins of 10-12% on $46B revenue would justify significantly higher valuation. Nike's 60-year brand moat and global distribution network remain intact assets that competitors cannot replicate.
thesis_continued: The bear case is severe: $1.5B in annual tariff headwinds from Vietnam and China sourcing are compressing gross margins below 41%, profit margins have collapsed to 4.8% from historical 12%, and the payout ratio exceeds 100% meaning the dividend is temporarily uncovered by earnings [1]. Revenue has contracted 2.7% over the trailing twelve months with NIKE Direct down 9% and Converse down 30%. Consecutive layoffs (1,600 in 2024, corporate cuts in 2025, 775 in January 2026) and the departures of the innovation chief and AI VP raise questions about whether Nike can innovate its way out of the decline while simultaneously cutting talent [2]. If tariffs escalate further or Hill's turnaround fails to restore mid-single-digit revenue growth by FY2027, the stock could face additional downside from the 24x forward multiple.
key_catalysts: Running category sustaining 20%+ growth through FY2026 Q3-Q4 driven by Nike Mind launch and renewed product innovation investment could signal the revenue inflection point
Tariff mitigation through supply chain diversification to Mexico and selective price increases beginning to offset the $1.5B annual gross margin headwind
Wholesale channel re-engagement with Foot Locker, Dick's, and Amazon driving incremental distribution revenue and NIKE Direct traffic through cross-channel exposure
key_risks: Tariff escalation beyond $1.5B annualized impact if Vietnam sourcing faces additional duties — Nike sources ~50% of footwear from Vietnam, creating concentrated geopolitical risk
On Running and Hoka continuing to gain running and lifestyle market share during the prolonged turnaround period, permanently reducing Nike's brand premium and pricing power
Payout ratio exceeding 100% at trough earnings — if margin recovery stalls, the 22-year dividend growth streak could be threatened, removing a key investor support for the stock
sources: NIKE Q3 FY2026 Earnings Release (quarter ending February 28, 2026) — SEC 8-K Filing, March 2026
Nike Leadership Changes, AI Strategy, and Innovation Chief Departure — Retail Dive, Chief AI Officer, Consumer Goods Technology, 2025-2026
Nike ESG Framework, Labor Practices, and Community Investment — Simply Wall St, KnowESG, 2025-2026
